worstCase: Compromised CONTROLLER executes doDelegateCall with a pre-deployed malicious contract address. The malicious contract runs in the ALMProxy storage context, calling approve and transfer on all ERC-20 tokens held by the proxy. In a single transaction, the entire ALMProxy TVL — potentially hundreds of millions — is transferred to the attacker address. No value limit, no address validation, no reversibility.

Compromised CONTROLLER executes doCall passing an ERC-20 token as target and approve(attacker, MAX) as calldata. The process can be repeated for each token in the proxy in successive calls. The complete absence of on-chain validation of target and calldata makes the vector identical to the Giddy pattern (2022 hack).

Same as doCall, plus native ETH drainage. ETH sent to a contract with no recovery function is permanently lost. In systems with cross-chain ALM that hold ETH for gas, the vector also destroys operational capacity.

Malicious roles contract returns true for any canCall. Any address can deposit/withdraw from the conduit without restriction, draining all D3M liquidity — potentially hundreds of millions of DAI/USDS deposited by MakerDAO.

New implementation includes an emergency function that transfers all vault assets to the attacker hardcoded address. Users with shares lose their USDC deposits immediately. The vault can operate on L1+L2 simultaneously — the damage can be parallel.

Crafted bitmap zeroes the liquidationThreshold for ETH. All positions using ETH as collateral become immediately liquidatable. Automated bots trigger cascade liquidations in the same block, leading to widespread bad debt.

New aToken implementation overrides burn() to transfer the underlying to the attacker address before returning. All withdrawals start draining to the attacker. The damage simultaneously affects all depositors of that asset.

New implementation returns balanceOf=0 for the attacker address, erasing their on-chain debt. The attacker had borrowed the maximum before the upgrade and now has intact collateral + zeroed debt = profit equal to the total borrowed amount.

Same vector as StableDebtToken, but the variable rate is used by most borrowers. New implementation manipulates the interest accrual rayMul to 100%/block — all active positions become instantly insolvent.

Compromised existing ward adds a malicious address as a new ward. New ward immediately executes upgradeToAndCall with a backdoored implementation. The rely is the entry vector — with no ward limit, the attacker maintains persistence even after the compromised ward is removed via deny.

Compromised SUBMISSION_ROLE queues: (1) setACLManager pointing to a contract that returns true for any role check; (2) setPoolImpl with backdoored implementation; (3) treasury transfer. After the delay, Executor executes automatically. The only defense is GUARDIAN_ROLE canceling before expiry.

Malicious token with a reentrant transfer() function listed with LTV=80%, no supply cap. Attacker deposits the token, manipulates Pool state via reentrancy during supply/liquidation, drains real assets. Tokens with non-standard ERC-20 behavior amplify the damage.

Compromised RISK_ADMIN sets liquidationThreshold=0 for WBTC. All WBTC positions become liquidatable. Automated liquidators execute in cascade in the same block. Partially liquidated positions generate unrecoverable bad debt.

Compromised governance replaces oracle with a contract returning inflated prices for ETH. Attacker deposits inflated ETH, borrows the maximum in stables, and exits. Alternatively, zeroed prices trigger liquidations of ALL positions across all assets simultaneously.

New ACLManager deployed with isPoolAdmin(), isRiskAdmin(), isEmergencyAdmin() returning true for any address. After replacement, any EOA can execute initReserves, updateAToken, setPoolPause. The entire protocol is compromised without needing legitimate roles.

Code executed via delegatecall in the Executor sets delay=0, removes GUARDIAN_ROLE, and transfers ETH. With delay=0, any action queued via queue is executable instantly — no defense window for the community to react.

The POOL key is replaced by a malicious contract that mimics the Pool interface but redirects all supply/borrow to drain the user. Legitimate frontends resolve the Pool address via PoolAddressesProvider — users unknowingly interact with the malicious contract.

Alternative backdoor to setPoolImpl — uses setAddressAsProxy(POOL, maliciousImpl) bypassing specific monitoring of setPoolImpl. New implementation controls 100% of deposits and loans. Can replace contracts without a dedicated upgrade.

New Pool implementation includes backdoorWithdraw(address to) with no restrictions. After upgrade, drains all assets from all reserves simultaneously. The Pool holds the real assets — a compromise is equivalent to compromising the entire protocol treasury.
New PoolConfigurator removes role checks from all functions. Any EOA can list malicious tokens, upgrade aTokens, and pause/unpause reserves. It is a precondition for multiple other attacks.

New implementation redirects withdraw/redeem to the attacker address. The vault continues appearing to function normally for deposits, attracting new deposits to the compromised contract while withdrawals are silently drained.
New implementation calls approve(attacker, MAX) on all buffer tokens. Attacker then executes transferFrom. Timing at the moment of maximum buffer balance (high cross-chain activity) maximizes damage.

Malicious bridge receives BRIDGE_ROLE. Immediately calls mintUnbacked(USDC, unbackedMintCap). Phantom aTokens are redeemed for real USDC from depositors. Maximum damage is capped by the unbackedMintCap — but in configurations with a high cap can reach tens of millions.

Malicious address with POOL_ADMIN executes sequence: updateAToken(WETH, backdooredImpl) + initReserves(maliciousToken) + rescueTokens. POOL_ADMIN is the role with the widest range of destructive actions — compromising it is functionally equivalent to compromising the entire protocol.

Malicious address becomes DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE. Executes setRoleAdmin(POOL_ADMIN_ROLE, ATTACKER_ROLE) + grantRole(POOL_ADMIN_ROLE, attacker). Full privilege escalation in 2 steps. It is the master key of the access control hierarchy.

Malicious new owner executes in sequence: setPoolImpl + setACLManager + setAddressAsProxy with backdoored implementations. The new owner requires no further approvals — it is the protocol master key. With Timelock as protection, the spell must be canceled before the delay expires.

Compromised governance executes setRoleAdmin(POOL_ADMIN_ROLE, ATTACKER_ROLE). Attacker with ATTACKER_ROLE then controls who holds POOL_ADMIN without needing DEFAULT_ADMIN. Covert privilege escalation — harder to detect than direct grantRole.

Compromised governance grants DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE to a malicious address via grantRole. With DEFAULT_ADMIN, the attacker controls the entire role hierarchy — can grant POOL_ADMIN, RISK_ADMIN, EMERGENCY_ADMIN to any address with no further approvals.

After renounceOwnership, no onlyOwner function can ever be called. Emergency upgrades are permanently impossible. If a critical vulnerability is discovered in the Pool, the protocol cannot fix it via proxy upgrade. Score 10 because all controls are present — the risk is the irreversible impact, not ease of exploitation.

Admin removes the transferAsset rate limit. Compromised RELAYER drains the entire ALMProxy in a single transaction — without the rate limit as containment, the maximum damage is the full proxy TVL, not just the per-period limit.
